summary: Gruber responds to Leo Laporte's criticism of Apple's iPhone 1.1.1 update, which removed third-party apps and broke unlocking hacks. He argues that Apple never sold the iPhone as a general-purpose computer, so users who hacked it were operating outside the intended use case. He challenges the characterization that Apple 'deleted' apps, explaining that iPhone updates are clean reinstalls of the entire system. While acknowledging the situation is frustrating, he rejects the framing that Apple acted nefariously, arguing that users who installed the update despite clear warnings bear responsibility for the consequences.
key_points: Apple isn't selling the iPhone as a general-purpose handheld computer, so expecting open-platform behavior is misplaced
iPhone updates are complete clean reinstalls, not targeted deletions of third-party apps — supporting hacked software would require engineering effort Apple never promised
The 1.1.1 update was voluntary and came with explicit warnings about bricking unlocked iPhones, so users who proceeded made an informed choice
Trustworthiness means doing what you say you will do, not doing whatever someone else wishes you would do
The situation is frustrating and arguably shitty, but framing it as Apple attacking its own customers is intellectually dishonest
money_quotes: The answer really is that the iPhone, as of today, isn't for you if you feel you must be able to install and modify software on it.
One of the advantages of a closed system is that it's far easier to create upgrades for.

To argue that Apple should have left third-party apps and hacks installed is to argue that Apple should have expended some amount of engineering resources to support something they neither endorsed nor encouraged in the first place.
If anything, the lesson to be drawn is that Apple is quite trustworthy — iPhone 1.1.1 is and does exactly what Apple has claimed the iPhone is and does.
To be trustworthy is to do what you say you will do; to do whatever someone else wishes you to do is to be obsequious.
Apple didn't kill or damage a single unlocked iPhone. They released a new software update which iPhone users had to agree to install.
It amounts to you killing your cow.
Your humble analogy-stretcher is himself quite enamored with the idea of allowing these clever cows to produce cheese.
key_insight: Apple's iPhone 1.1.1 update wasn't an act of betrayal against hackers — it was Apple doing exactly what it always said it would do, and users who chose to install it on hacked devices bear the consequences of their own informed decision.
